**Alfred Louis Kroeber** ([/ˈkroʊbər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KROH-bər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); June 11, 1876 – October 5, 1960) was an American [cultural anthropologist](/wiki/Cultural_anthropologist "Cultural anthropologist"). He received his PhD under [Franz Boas](/wiki/Franz_Boas "Franz Boas") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in 1901, the first doctorate in [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") awarded by Columbia. He was also the first professor appointed to the Department of Anthropology at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley").[[3]](#cite_note-ucb-anthro-history-3) He played an integral role in the early days of its [Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/Phoebe_A._Hearst_Museum_of_Anthropology "Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology"), where he served as director from 1909 through 1947.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Kroeber provided detailed information about [Ishi](/wiki/Ishi "Ishi"), the last surviving member of the [Yahi people](/wiki/Yahi_people "Yahi people"), whom he studied over a period of years. He was the father of the author [Ursula K. Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin").

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Kroeber&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Ishi.jpg/250px-Ishi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ishi.jpg)

Kroeber (left) with [Ishi](/wiki/Ishi "Ishi") in 1911

Kroeber was born in [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"), to parents of German Protestant origin. His mother, Johanna Mueller (or Muller[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber19705-5)), was an American descended from [German immigrants after 1848](/wiki/Forty-eighters "Forty-eighters"),[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlson20094-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlson19819–10-7) sister to three brothers, including Hermann (the father of [Hermann Joseph Muller](/wiki/Hermann_Joseph_Muller "Hermann Joseph Muller")) and Otto (the grandfather of [Herbert J. Muller](/wiki/Herbert_J._Muller "Herbert J. Muller")) [[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9). Kroeber's father, Florenz (or Florence[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber19705-5)) Friederick Martin Kroeber, came to the United States from Germany at the age of ten, with his parents and family,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber19705-5) and became an importer of French clocks as his wife's father, Nicholas Mueller.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKroeber19706-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarlson198110–11-12) The family belonged to a German-American milieu that was upper middle-class, classical and rationalistic, and schooled in the German intellectual tradition.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Alfred's family moved into New York City when Alfred was quite young, and he was tutored and attended private schools there. He had three younger siblings and all had scholarly interests. The family was bilingual, speaking German at home, and Kroeber also began to study [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") in school, beginning a lifelong interest in languages.[[2]](#cite_note-Steward-2) He attended [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College,_Columbia_University "Columbia College, Columbia University") at the age of 16, joining the [Philolexian Society](/wiki/Philolexian_Society "Philolexian Society") and earning an BA in English in 1896 and an MA in Romantic drama in 1897. Changing fields to the new one of [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology"), he received his PhD under [Franz Boas](/wiki/Franz_Boas "Franz Boas") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in 1901,[[15]](#cite_note-15) basing his 28-page dissertation on decorative symbolism on his field work among the [Arapaho](/wiki/Arapaho "Arapaho"). It was the first doctorate in anthropology awarded by Columbia.

Kroeber spent most of his career in California, primarily at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"). He was both a professor of Anthropology and the Director of what was then the University of California Museum of Anthropology (now the [Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/Phoebe_A._Hearst_Museum_of_Anthropology "Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology")). The anthropology department's headquarters building at the University of California was named Kroeber Hall in his honor, before being unnamed January 26, 2021, in order to "help Berkeley recognize a challenging part of our history, while better supporting the diversity of today's academic community."[[16]](#cite_note-16) He was associated with Berkeley until his retirement in 1946. He died in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") on October 5, 1960.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Kroeber&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Kroeber married Henriette Rothschild in 1906. She contracted [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") and died in 1913, after several years of illness.[[2]](#cite_note-Steward-2)

In 1926 he married again, to [Theodora Kracaw Brown](/wiki/Theodora_Kroeber "Theodora Kroeber"), a widow who had been a student in one of his graduate seminars.[[2]](#cite_note-Steward-2) They had two children: [Karl Kroeber](/wiki/Karl_Kroeber "Karl Kroeber"), a literary critic, and the science fiction writer [Ursula Kroeber Le Guin](/wiki/Ursula_K._Le_Guin "Ursula K. Le Guin"). In addition, Alfred adopted Theodora's sons by her first marriage, Ted and [Clifton Brown](/w/index.php?title=Clifton_Kroeber&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clifton Kroeber (page does not exist)"), who both took his surname.

In 2003, Clifton and Karl Kroeber published a book of essays on [Ishi](/wiki/Ishi "Ishi")'s story, which they co-edited, called, *Ishi in Three Centuries*.[[18]](#cite_note-18) This is the first scholarly book on Ishi to contain essays by Native American writers and academics.

After her husband's death, Theodora Kroeber wrote a biography of him, titled *[Alfred Kroeber: A Personal Configuration](/wiki/Alfred_Kroeber:_A_Personal_Configuration "Alfred Kroeber: A Personal Configuration")*. It was published by the [University of California Press](/wiki/University_of_California_Press "University of California Press") in 1970. [David G. Mandelbaum](/wiki/David_G._Mandelbaum "David G. Mandelbaum"), a cultural anthropologist and former colleague of Alfred,[[19]](#cite_note-19) stated that this biography was just as important a work from an anthropologist's perspective as *[Ishi in Two Worlds](/wiki/Ishi_in_Two_Worlds "Ishi in Two Worlds")*.[[20]](#cite_note-Mandelbaum-20)

## Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Kroeber&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Influence")]

Although he is known primarily as a [cultural anthropologist](/wiki/Cultural_anthropology "Cultural anthropology"), he did significant work in [archaeology](/wiki/Archaeology "Archaeology") and [anthropological linguistics](/wiki/Anthropological_linguistics "Anthropological linguistics"), and he contributed to anthropology by making connections between archaeology and culture. He conducted excavations in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"). In Peru he helped found the [Institute for Andean Studies](/w/index.php?title=Institute_for_Andean_Studies&action=edit&redlink=1 "Institute for Andean Studies (page does not exist)") (IAS) with the Peruvian anthropologist [Julio C. Tello](/wiki/Julio_C._Tello "Julio C. Tello") and other major scholars.

Kroeber and his students did important work collecting cultural data on western tribes of [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"). The work done in preserving information about Californian tribes appeared in *Handbook of the Indians of California* (1925). In that book, Kroeber first described a pattern in Californian groups where a social unit was smaller and less hierarchically organized than a [tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe"),[[21]](#cite_note-kroeber1976handbook-21)[[22]](#cite_note-golla2011-22) which was elaborated upon in *The Patwin and their Neighbors*[[23]](#cite_note-kroeberpatwin-23) in which Kroeber first coined the term "**tribelet**" to describe this level of organization. Kroeber is credited with developing the concepts of [culture area](/wiki/Culture_area "Culture area"), cultural configuration (*Cultural and Natural Areas of Native North America*, 1939), and **cultural fatigue** (*Anthropology*, 1963).

Kroeber influenced many of his contemporaries in his views as a cultural historian. During his lifetime, he was known as the "Dean of American Anthropologists". Kroeber and [Roland B. Dixon](/wiki/Roland_Burrage_Dixon "Roland Burrage Dixon") were very influential in the genetic classification of [Native American languages](/wiki/Native_American_languages "Native American languages") in North America, being responsible for theoretical groupings such as [Penutian](/wiki/Penutian_languages "Penutian languages") and [Hokan](/wiki/Hokan_languages "Hokan languages"), based on common languages.

He is noted for working with [Ishi](/wiki/Ishi "Ishi"), who was claimed to be the last California [Yahi](/wiki/Yahi "Yahi") Indian. (Ishi may have been of mixed ethnic heritage, with a father from the [Wintun](/wiki/Wintun "Wintun"), [Maidu](/wiki/Maidu "Maidu") or [Nomlaki](/wiki/Nomlaki "Nomlaki") tribes.)[[24]](#cite_note-24) His second wife, [Theodora Kracaw Kroeber](/wiki/Theodora_Kroeber "Theodora Kroeber"), wrote a well-known biography of Ishi, *[Ishi in Two Worlds](/wiki/Ishi_in_Two_Worlds "Ishi in Two Worlds")*. Kroeber's relationship with Ishi was the subject of a film, *[The Last of His Tribe](/wiki/The_Last_of_His_Tribe "The Last of His Tribe")* (1992), starring [Jon Voight](/wiki/Jon_Voight "Jon Voight") as Kroeber and [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene_(actor) "Graham Greene (actor)") as Ishi.[[25]](#cite_note-LAT-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Fleras-26)

Kroeber's textbook, *Anthropology* (1923, 1948), was widely used for many years. In the late 1940s, it was one of ten books required as reading for all students during their first year at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"). His book, *Configurations of Cultural Growth* (1944), had a lasting impact on social scientific research on [genius](/wiki/Genius "Genius") and [greatness](/wiki/Greatness "Greatness"); Kroeber believed that genius arose out of culture at particular times, rather than holding to "the great man" theory.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Kroeber's childhood friend Carl Alsberg described him as a "good listener" and able "to be objective, to see the other point of view, to penetrate behind another person's behavior to his underlying thought [...] These traits indicate a sincerity and simplicity of character that primitive peoples sense at once and to which they respond by giving their confidence."[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)

From 1920 to 1923 Kroeber conducted an active practice as a lay psychoanalyst, with an office in San Francisco.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)

## Indian land claims

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Kroeber&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Indian land claims")]

Kroeber served early on as the plaintiffs' director of research in *Indians of California v. the United States*, a land claim case.[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Omer Stewart](/wiki/Omer_Stewart "Omer Stewart") of the University of Colorado served as associate director. [Ralph Beals](/wiki/Ralph_Beals "Ralph Beals") of the University of California, Los Angeles, served as director of research for the federal government in the case. Both men were former students of Kroeber.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Kroeber's impact on the [Indian Claims Commission](/wiki/Indian_Claims_Commission "Indian Claims Commission") may well have established the way expert witnesses presented testimony before the tribunal.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Several of his former students also served as expert witnesses; for instance, Stewart directed the plaintiff research for the [Ute](/wiki/Ute_people "Ute people") and for the [Shoshone](/wiki/Shoshone "Shoshone") peoples.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Awards and honors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Kroeber&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Awards and honors")]

* Fellow of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences") (1912)[[33]](#cite_note-AAAS-33)
* Kroeber received five honorary degrees (Yale, California, Harvard, Columbia, Chicago)
* He was awarded two gold medals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* He held honorary membership in 16 scientific societies.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* President of the [American Anthropological Association](/wiki/American_Anthropological_Association "American Anthropological Association") (1917–1918)
* Member of the United States [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") (1928)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* Member of the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") (1941)[[36]](#cite_note-36)
